title: Puppy Training Checklist (2026) – Every Supply & Milestone You Need
excerpt: Complete puppy training checklist with supplies, milestones, and timelines. Everything first-time puppy parents need from day one.
quickAnswer: A complete puppy training checklist includes a crate, enzymatic cleaner, training treats, a 6-foot leash, and a harness. Start potty training immediately, introduce basic commands by week two, and begin socialization before 14 weeks.
whoThisIsFor: First-time puppy parents who want a structured plan
Experienced owners adopting a new breed
Families preparing their home for a puppy

sections.heading: Essential Puppy Training Supplies
sections.content:
Before your puppy arrives, stock up on these non-negotiable supplies:

**Crate** — Wire crates with divider panels grow with your puppy. Size it so your pup can stand, turn, and lie down comfortably. Too large and they'll potty in the corner.

**Enzymatic cleaner** — Regular cleaners mask the smell to humans but not dogs. Enzymatic formulas break down urine proteins completely, preventing repeat accidents in the same spot.

**Training treats** — Small, soft, smelly treats work best. Your puppy should be able to eat them in under 2 seconds so you don't lose the training moment. Look for treats under 5 calories each.

**6-foot leash** — Skip retractable leashes entirely during training. A standard 6-foot nylon or biothane leash gives you consistent control without confusing your puppy about boundaries.

**Front-clip harness** — For puppies over 12 weeks, a front-clip harness redirects pulling force toward you rather than rewarding forward momentum. [Shop our training harnesses](/collections/dog-leash-control).

**Treat pouch** — Fumbling in your pockets kills training momentum. A clip-on treat pouch keeps rewards accessible for instant reinforcement.

sections.heading: Week-by-Week Training Milestones
sections.content:
**Week 1–2: Foundation**
- Establish potty schedule (every 2 hours + after meals, naps, play)
- Introduce crate as safe space (never punishment)
- Begin name recognition with treats
- Start handling exercises (touch paws, ears, mouth)

**Week 3–4: Basic Commands**
- Sit (lure method: treat over nose, mark the moment)
- Come (short distances indoors only)
- Leave it (start with low-value items)
- Begin leash introduction indoors

**Week 5–8: Building Duration**
- Down command
- Stay (start at 3 seconds, build to 30)
- Loose-leash walking in yard
- First [puppy socialization](/guides/puppy-socialization-guide) experiences

**Week 9–12: Proofing**
- Practice commands with mild distractions
- Graduate to outdoor walks
- Begin impulse control games (wait at doors, wait for food)
- Introduce grooming routine

Every puppy learns at their own pace. If your pup struggles with a step, go back to the previous milestone and build more confidence before advancing.

sections.heading: Potty Training Essentials
sections.content:
Potty training is every new puppy parent's top concern — and the most common reason for frustration.

**The golden rule**: puppies can hold it roughly one hour per month of age. A 3-month-old puppy needs to go out every 3 hours maximum.

**High-success schedule**:
- Immediately after waking
- 15 minutes after eating or drinking
- After play sessions
- Before crate time
- Last thing before bed

**Mark and reward**: The instant your puppy finishes going outside, mark with "yes!" and deliver a high-value treat. This timing is everything — even a 3-second delay weakens the association.

**Accident protocol**: If you catch them mid-accident, calmly interrupt and carry them outside. Clean with enzymatic cleaner. Never punish after the fact — your puppy literally cannot connect the punishment to an accident that happened even 30 seconds ago.

For a deeper dive into potty training methods, see our [complete potty training guide](/guides/dog-potty-training-complete-guide). Browse [potty training supplies](/collections/dog-potty-training) designed for fast results.

sections.heading: Common Puppy Training Mistakes
sections.content:
**Inconsistency** — The #1 training killer. If "off the couch" applies Monday but not Sunday, your puppy learns that rules are optional. Every household member must enforce the same rules.

**Training sessions too long** — Puppies have the attention span of a goldfish. Keep sessions to 3–5 minutes, 3–4 times daily. Five focused minutes beats a frustrating 30-minute session.

**Waiting too long to socialize** — The critical socialization window closes around 14–16 weeks. Every positive new experience before that window shapes a confident adult dog. After it closes, new experiences require much more careful introduction. Read our [socialization guide](/guides/puppy-socialization-guide).

**Skipping crate training** — Many owners feel guilty about crates. But a properly introduced crate becomes your puppy's safe haven — not a jail. Crate-trained puppies potty train faster, experience less separation anxiety, and are safer when unsupervised.

**Using the wrong treats** — Dry, crunchy treats take too long to eat and break training flow. Use soft, pea-sized treats your puppy can swallow in one second.

faq.question: What age should I start training my puppy?
faq.answer: Start training the day your puppy comes home, typically at 8 weeks. Begin with name recognition, potty training, and crate introduction. Formal obedience commands like sit and down can begin at 8–10 weeks.

faq.question: How long does it take to fully train a puppy?
faq.answer: Basic obedience takes 4–6 months of consistent daily practice. Potty training takes 4–8 weeks for most breeds. Full reliability in all environments typically takes 12–18 months of ongoing reinforcement.

faq.question: What supplies do I need for puppy training?
faq.answer: Essential supplies include a properly sized crate, enzymatic cleaner, soft training treats, a 6-foot leash, a front-clip harness, and a treat pouch. Budget approximately $150–$200 for quality starter supplies.

faq.question: Should I use punishment in puppy training?
faq.answer: No. Punishment-based methods increase fear, anxiety, and aggression. Positive reinforcement — rewarding desired behaviors — is more effective and builds a trusting relationship with your puppy.
